skill.name: response-tone-polisher
skill.description: Polishes response letters by transforming defensive or harsh language into professional, courteous academic prose while preserving the author's scientific position.
skill.summary: Transforms defensive peer review response drafts into polished, professional academic prose.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: All 80 static analysis findings were evaluated and dismissed as false positives. The 51 'shell backtick execution' findings were markdown code fences in SKILL.md and tone_patterns.md, not actual command execution. The 10 'weak cryptographic algorithm' findings matched no cryptographic code - they were documentation text and JSON data. The 3 'path traversal' findings were a string slice operation and security checklist warnings against traversal. The 8 'system reconnaissance' findings were documentation headers and regex patterns. All file operations are legitimate CLI read/write operations. The only real risks are standard Python file I/O and the presence of a main.py script, which are documented and expected for this tool.

low_findings.title: Markdown code blocks flagged as shell backtick execution
low_findings.description: 51 findings across SKILL.md and references/tone_patterns.md were flagged as Ruby/shell backtick execution. All are standard markdown triple-backtick code blocks used to display example commands and response templates. No actual shell execution occurs in code. Confirmed false positive.

low_findings.title: Security checklist items flagged as path traversal
low_findings.description: Two findings in SKILL.md at lines 260 and 263 flagged '../' as path traversal. These are security checklist items explicitly warning against path traversal ('No unauthorized file system access (../)' and 'Input file paths validated (no ../ traversal)'). The code is documenting security best practices, not performing traversal. Confirmed false positive.

low_findings.title: Display string formatting flagged as path traversal
low_findings.description: String slicing operation imp['original_phrase'][:50] in scripts/main.py:530 was flagged as path traversal. This truncates text for display output, not file system navigation. Confirmed false positive.

low_findings.title: Documentation text flagged as weak cryptographic algorithm
low_findings.description: 10 findings across multiple files flagged text content as weak cryptographic algorithms. No cryptographic operations or algorithm references exist in the codebase. Matched content includes JSON data fields, markdown documentation headers, and argparse description strings. Confirmed false positive.

low_findings.title: Legitimate file I/O operations flagged as security risk
low_findings.description: Two file write operations in scripts/main.py (lines 552 and 644) were flagged. These are standard CLI tool operations that save user-requested output to user-specified file paths. One uses input() for filename, the other uses the --output argument. Expected behavior for a text processing tool. Confirmed false positive.

low_findings.title: Documentation and comments flagged as system reconnaissance
low_findings.description: 8 findings flagged documentation text, markdown headers, and regex pattern strings as system reconnaissance. The flagged content includes section headers ('Over-Apologizing'), code comments, and academic regex patterns for detecting tone issues. No system enumeration or reconnaissance behavior exists. Confirmed false positive.

low_findings.title: GitHub URL in attribution flagged as hardcoded network endpoint
low_findings.description: Two findings on SKILL.md line 7 flagged the GitHub repository URL. This is a standard open-source attribution link displaying the source repository for the skill. No network calls are made by the code. Confirmed false positive.

content.user_title: Polish Your Peer Review Response Letters
content.value_statement: Researchers often struggle with defensive or harsh language in draft responses to peer reviewers. This skill transforms confrontational drafts into professional, courteous academic prose while preserving the author's scientific position and integrity.
actual_capabilities: Detects defensive, confrontational, dismissive, and blame-shifting language patterns in draft responses
Transforms harsh statements into courteous academic prose using a curated library of polite expressions
Preserves the author's scientific position while improving professional delivery
Supports three response scenarios: accepting suggestions, partial acceptance, and respectful decline
Provides tone analysis scores and a detailed list of before-and-after improvements
Adapts polish intensity with light, moderate, and heavy levels based on user preference
limitations: Does not verify the scientific accuracy or validity of the author's claims
May over-soften language in some cases; authors should verify their position remains clearly communicated
Best suited for English-language academic writing; other languages may not produce consistent results
Cannot guarantee reviewer acceptance or satisfaction with the polished response

use_cases.title: Responding to Critical Reviewer Comments
use_cases.description: Transform a defensive or frustrated draft response to a harsh reviewer comment into a professional, diplomatic reply that addresses the concern without confrontation.
use_cases.target_user: Academic researchers and scientists responding to peer review feedback

use_cases.title: Preparing Manuscript Revision Letters
use_cases.description: Polish point-by-point responses to multiple reviewer comments in revision letters, ensuring each response maintains a consistent professional tone throughout.
use_cases.target_user: Graduate students and postdoctoral fellows preparing journal revision submissions

use_cases.title: Improving Collaborative Feedback
use_cases.description: Refine language used in collaborative internal review settings where direct criticism may damage working relationships within research teams or lab groups.
use_cases.target_user: Research team leads and principal investigators providing constructive feedback to colleagues

prompt_templates.title: Quick Tone Polish
prompt_templates.prompt:
Polish the following draft response to a peer reviewer. Make it professional and courteous while preserving the scientific position.

Reviewer comment: [paste reviewer comment here]

Draft response: [paste your draft response here]
prompt_templates.scenario: User needs a quick one-shot polish of a single response

prompt_templates.title: Detailed Tone Transformation with Tracking
prompt_templates.prompt:
I need to polish my response to a peer reviewer. Apply moderate polish level.

Reviewer comment: [paste reviewer comment here]

Draft response: [paste your draft response here]

For each change you make, show the original phrase and the replacement. Explain why the change improves the tone.
prompt_templates.scenario: User wants to understand each transformation and learn from the process

prompt_templates.title: Heavy Polish for Confrontational Draft
prompt_templates.prompt:
My draft response is too confrontational. Apply heavy polishing.

Reviewer comment: [paste reviewer comment here]

Draft response: [paste your draft response here]

Transform all defensive language. Add appropriate acknowledgments. Include polite academic expressions. Ensure the final response still firmly maintains our scientific position on the core issue.
prompt_templates.scenario: User has written an overly defensive response that needs significant rewriting

prompt_templates.title: Complete Revision Letter Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt:
Help me write a professional response letter to peer reviewers. I have the following comments and draft responses:

[Paste all reviewer comments and your draft responses, numbered]

For each comment:
1. Identify tone issues in my draft
2. Provide a polished version
3. Explain what changed and why

Use this approach: acknowledge the reviewer, address concerns respectfully, and maintain scientific integrity.
prompt_templates.scenario: User needs a complete revision letter addressing multiple review points

output_examples.input:
Reviewer: The sample size is too small for meaningful conclusions.
Draft Response: This is standard in our field. The reviewer is wrong.
output_examples.output: We appreciate the reviewer’s concern regarding sample size. While we acknowledge that larger samples can provide greater statistical power, our sample size is consistent with established conventions in this field and meets the requirements for adequate power analysis as detailed in the Methods section. We have added a brief note clarifying this to enhance transparency.

output_examples.input:
Reviewer: Your methodology is fundamentally flawed.
Draft Response: No it's not. We did everything correctly.
output_examples.output: We thank the reviewer for this careful examination of our methodology. We respectfully offer additional context regarding our methodological choices, which were selected based on established protocols in the field. We have expanded the Methods section to provide further detail and justification for our approach.

output_examples.input:
Reviewer: This analysis is incomplete without additional experiments.
Draft Response: We can't do more experiments. This is not our fault.
output_examples.output: We appreciate the reviewer’s suggestion regarding additional experiments. We acknowledge this limitation and recognize that further work would strengthen the study. However, the current scope and available resources constrain our ability to conduct additional experiments at this time. We have added appropriate caveats regarding this limitation to the Discussion section.

best_practices: Always review the polished output to verify your scientific position is still accurately and firmly represented.
Provide the full reviewer comment along with your draft for more context-aware and accurate tone transformation.
Use moderate polish level as the default and switch to heavy only when the original draft contains highly confrontational language.
anti_patterns: Do not use heavy polish on responses that require firm, evidence-based scientific pushback, as it may weaken your position unnecessarily.
Do not skip human review of the output assuming the tool handles all nuance correctly, especially for complex or sensitive disagreements.
Do not include personal identifying information or confidential data in the drafts you submit for polishing.

faq.question: Does this skill change the scientific meaning of my response?
faq.answer: The skill preserves your scientific position. It only changes the tone and delivery of the language, not the factual or scientific content. You should always review the output to confirm your meaning is intact.

faq.question: Which polish level should I use for most responses?
faq.answer: Moderate polish is recommended for most situations. It softens defensive language while keeping your position clear. Use light for minor adjustments and heavy only when the original draft is very confrontational.

faq.question: Can I use this skill for non-academic writing?
faq.answer: The skill is designed specifically for academic peer review responses. While the tone transformation may work on other professional writing, the polite expressions and patterns are tailored to academic contexts.

faq.question: How does the tone analysis scoring work?
faq.answer: The tool calculates a defensiveness score from 0 to 1 based on the number and severity of detected tone issues relative to the text length. It also computes a politeness score based on positive and negative language indicators.

faq.question: Does this skill make network calls or send my data externally?
faq.answer: No. The skill runs entirely locally. Your reviewer comments and draft responses are processed on your machine and are not sent to any external server or service.

faq.question: Can it handle multiple reviewer comments in one pass?
faq.answer: Yes. You can paste multiple reviewer comments and your draft responses together. The tool will process the entire text. For best results, format each comment and response pair clearly.
